overview.summary: Codex Regius is not the absolute oldest surviving piece of Eddic poetry — runic inscriptions and wooden sticks can be centuries earlier — but it is the most complete and best-preserved medieval vellum witness for the Poetic Edda. AM 748 I 4to and scattered fragments supplement it; scholars treat GKS 2365 4to as the principal text.
older_traditions: Bergen and Trondheim rune sticks (12th–14th c.)

artifact_categories.label: Rune sticks (træstikker)
artifact_categories.description: Medieval wooden sticks from Norwegian towns, often with Eddic lines — especially Hávamál and Sigurðr-cycle formulae.

artifact_categories.label: Runestones & image stones
artifact_categories.description: Granite and rock carvings with mythological names, poetic phrases, or heroic scenes parallel to Eddic narrative.

artifact_categories.label: Galdr / magical runes
artifact_categories.description: Short protective or cursing formulas showing live magical use of the same language register as the codex.

artifacts.name: Rök runestone (Ög 136)
artifacts.note: Longest known Elder Futhark inscription; alliterative narrative with mythological names (e.g. Þórr). Thematic overlap with Eddic cosmogony — not a direct copy of Codex Regius text.

artifacts.name: Bergen stick P27 (Bryggen)
artifacts.note: Contains Hávamál lines closely matching the Codex Regius tradition — among the strongest manuscript–runic textual bridges.
artifacts.reference: N B511 corpus; McLeod & Mees, Runic Amulets

artifacts.name: Bergen stick N B511
artifacts.note: Famous stick with Hávamál st. 77 region — 'at skal ráða…' formula widely cited in Eddic–runic studies.

artifacts.name: Ramsund carving (Sigurðr)
artifacts.note: Visual narrative of Sigurðr, Reginn, and Fáfnir — parallels heroic lays preserved in Codex Regius.

artifacts.name: Gök runestone
artifacts.note: Þórr fishing for Miðgarðsormr — scene echoed in Eddic Þórr poems.

artifacts.name: Eggja stone
artifacts.note: Complex inscription with possible mythological vocabulary; debated reading but important pre-codex Norse register.

artifacts.name: Norwegian galdr sticks (various)
artifacts.note: Protective and cursing formulas using the same alliterative metre and wisdom-register as Hávamál.

stanza_links.cr_text: Hlióðs bið ek allar helgar kindir
stanza_links.note: Opening seeress formula — oral-formulaic; Rök stone shares mythological narrative register, not direct line.

stanza_links.cr_text: At skal ráða, ef þú regin villu / að þér gagnstæð finnask
stanza_links.note: Classic Bergen stick parallel — one of the closest runic witnesses to Codex Regius wording.

stanza_links.cr_text: Áðr gangi fram / stumk dvergr und asa
stanza_links.note: Wisdom and galdr-adjacent metre; compare magical stick formulas from Bergen corpus.
Heroic stone carvings depict the same legend cluster as Codex Regius heroic lays.
Gök stone fishing scene parallels Þórr–Miðgarðsormr/Eddic Þórr traditions.

poem_summaries.summary: Cosmogonic and prophetic — compare Rök stone and Eggja stone for pre-codex mythological register.
Highest density of rune-stick parallels in the corpus; Bergen sticks are nearly identical for multiple stanzas.
Heroic image stones (Ramsund, related carvings) narrate the same legend in visual form.
